nativeRange.habitat: The Pollenei Grouper is found across scattered oceanic islands of the Indo-Pacific, generally absent from continental coasts and major archipelagos. In the wild it tends to inhabit clear, open-water reef slopes and steep drop-offs, most commonly at depths between 25 and 120 m, though the species has been recorded as shallow as 10 m. It is closely associated with caves, overhangs, and crevices, and is rarely observed in shallower than about 30 m.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a range of large meaty marine foods including krill, raw shrimp, chopped marine fish, squid, clam and similar items. Frozen mysis or enriched brine, fiddler crabs and other small meaty live foods have been reported to be readily accepted. Feeder fish are best avoided as a primary food source; feeding every other day to a few times per week is typically recommended for adults.

goodWith: robust predatory tankmates of comparable size

imageCandidates.notes: Marc Taquet (IFREMER), 3072x2048. Sharp, well-lit live Cephalopholis polleni on black background showing full lateral profile with the classic yellow-orange body, violet wavy stripes and blue head spotting; striking and correctly identified.

Hugues Evano (IFREMER), 2560x1536. Sharp full-lateral view clearly showing the species' color and stripe pattern; caveat: a freshly-caught specimen on a white studio background (not a live in-situ shot), included as reference filler.

summary: The Pollenei Grouper (Cephalopholis polleni) is a medium-large predator from deep reef slopes of scattered Indo-Pacific islands, widely considered aggressive and best kept in a large fish-only display with robust, similarly sized tankmates.
careNotes:
The Pollenei Grouper (Cephalopholis polleni) — also known in the trade as the Harlequin Hind, Polleni Grouper, or Harlequin Rockcod — is a medium-large predatory reef fish distributed across scattered oceanic islands of the Indo-Pacific, from Comoros and Mauritius east through the Maldives to the Line Islands and French Polynesia. It is generally absent from continental Indian Ocean coasts, Australia, New Guinea, and the major Indonesian archipelagos. In the wild it tends to occur along clear-water drop-offs at moderate-to-deep reef depths and is commonly associated with caves and overhangs.

It is generally considered intermediate to keep, with the primary challenges being its eventual size, heavy meaty waste output, and pronounced predatory behavior. It is broadly accepted as a solitary ambush predator that feeds on fishes and crustaceans at the top of the food chain, and it will readily eat any tankmate it can fit in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp and small fish.

A minimum tank of around 1,000 L (approximately 265 gal) is recommended to provide adequate swimming volume, footprint, and waste capacity for a fully grown adult; sources differ on the lower bound, with some suggesting smaller tanks, but erring toward the larger figure is generally considered prudent. A long aquarium with substantial live-rock structure arranged to create caves and overhangs, plus ample open swimming space along the front, is generally advised.

Like most groupers in the genus Cephalopholis, this species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish may transition to male as it grows. Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. It has no venomous spines and is not considered a notable escape risk, though a secure lid is generally sensible for any large active predator. Reef compatibility is commonly listed as 'with caution': adult specimens pose minimal direct threat to stony and soft corals but will readily consume mobile reef invertebrates and small fish.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Cephalopholis polleni has not been documented in hobby literature. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish may transition to male as it grows; specific spawning behavior in the wild is poorly characterized.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: 250 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025, care level moderate, temperament aggressive, reef safe with caution, max size 14 in (~36 cm), family listed as Serranidae (older classification).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae / Perciformes), maximum size (43 cm TL), depth and temperature ranges (20.7–28.2°C, mean 26.5°C), and global distribution. FishBase wild temperature mean is slightly higher than aquarium-hobby ranges; reported as Least Concern (IUCN 2018).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (16 in / ~41 cm), minimum tank size (150 gal / ~568 L — notably smaller than LiveAquaria's 250 gal), care level (listed easy — averaged with LiveAquaria 'moderate' to give 'intermediate'), water parameters (matched LiveAquaria), temperament (aggressive), diet and feeding-frequency guidance. Tank-size disagreement biased toward the larger figure per playbook.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. In the wild, as a protogynous hermaphrodite, dominant females may transition to male as they grow.
